segments.text: Hi everyone, thank you for joining us and we're here to talk today about how we can help
close the loop between the design and construction phases on different projects and how systems
such as Qflow and Autodesk are working together in a wide area system to use AI and different
technologies to make sure that what we're designing is actually what we're building in reality.
So to introduce ourselves, my name is Jay and I'm one of the co-founders and CPO at Qflow
and I'm joined by Marta who is the Director of Sustainability Solutions at Autodesk.
So to frame the problem a little bit, despite popular belief, what we actually build in
reality often doesn't match up with our original design intent and where there are discrepancies
between what we think we're going to be building and both in terms of the types of materials
that are going into the buildings, the time frames at which that's being built and different
changes that can happen between both the design phase and the construction phase means
that in the messiness that occurs between those stages, there's often different mistakes,
material wastage that happens and risks that evolve when we start deviating between the
original design intent and the reality. And to give us an example of problems like this,
so at Qflow we've been working with a series of different GCs, developers, asset owners,
over time. And one of the stories that was told to us a while back before we deployed
on a particular project was a warehouse development that was being built and during the construction
phase the wrong time of agriculture had been brought onto the site through no manantent
but had been installed without being noticed and the warehouse came into fruition. It was
then tentative and operations were made for about six months until unfortunately ground
pressures were found and it meant that part of the warehouse had to be taken out of action
to redo part of the flooring where the aggra-gris was put into place and where the ground
depressions had occurred. Now at the time when the aggra-gris was delivered nobody thought much of
it and those assumptions were added being the correct grade and so the problem went unnoticed
until it began a problem further down the line. And unfortunately in that particular instance
because the section of the warehouse had been taken out of action it resulted in a large claim
from the tenants to the warehouse owner surplus of one million euros in lost income due to the
fact that those reworks then needed to happen. And unfortunately this is not a uncommon situation
uncommon story. Whilst the specific examples may look quite different on different projects
there are too many different examples of where different ceiling tiles had been installed or
other substances that have been prohibited in the site had managed to find their way in
or the rock concrete in fact has even been installed and then had to be broken out later once it was
once it was realised that it wasn't the spec or the grade that was desired.
And the reason that those problems exist is because on different kinds of schemes we've got anywhere
between tens hundreds or even thousands of deliveries and materials that are being delivered
onto these job sites every day and unfortunately we still operate you under a very analog system
where every delivery that's made is accompanied by a delivery receipt or a delivery ticket either
paper-based or in PDF form but ultimately information is contained within a series of documents
which describe exactly the item and the thing that's been delivered versus what has been designed for
what has been even ordered or procured and there are changes that can happen in between that process
things may substituted and so for an individual human or a team on site it is impossible to manage
all these deliveries coming on cross check all of that paperwork and ensure that everything is meeting
the specifications or the design that was originally intended and so information is naturally missed
changes aren't captured and what it does unfortunately result in is ineffective quality control over
these different kinds of materials and where those problems can later manifest.
So when we scale this up from individual materials or products we delivered to entire buildings
or structures or infrastructure it results in a huge disconnect between this design intent that
is within our control and can be specified to the nth degree which is then handled over to GCs
and subcontractors and supply chain and loads of different parties involved in the build process
where mistakes do happen and things change and so the materials that we thought were going to be
put into a building don't necessarily have up being ones that are installed and this is the specific
problem that a key flow we've wanted to tackle in how can we transform material tracking from this
quite analog traditional workflow into a digital one so that we can not only get better data and
information on what's actually being installed in these buildings but how can we serve as risks
in a much quicker fashion such that we can prevent the mistakes and prevent the discrepancies
that happen between those two phases and so a lot of this is around data continuity in different
phases of project from planning or through to the build and final operation.
So it would like to pass up a two martyrs talk a little bit further around what those phases might look
like. Thanks Jade. Yeah at Autodesk we think about the life cycle of design and make tools and workflows
in an ideal state and ideally to optimize cost and carbon and other kind of impacts across the
project life cycle we would love to see this sort of circular view of everything working together
achieving sustainable outcomes especially depends on the integrative and collaborative dynamics
between the processes and the people and the technologies especially across this life cycle.
However as you see in the sort of orange jagged line there the current state when it comes to
data flow and data management is that the information that flows across project life cycles is pretty
jagged and inconsistent there's different systems and stakeholders and as Jade mentioned there's a
mix between analog and digital solutions so today we know that stakeholders manage this data in a
really disconnected way and there's data loss at different points across this life cycle. This
disconnect prohibits sustainability outcomes and compromises the environmental targets we're setting.
Thank you master and absolutely anything to bring that back to the current process in terms of
what this looks like on silent and why these disconnects happen between those phases is that when
we think about how projects currently move between design and construction we have incredibly
complex and comprehensive information data and magic systems held within tools like REVIT
and then when we come to the building phase we've got a whole suite of tools and intelligence that's
held within Autodesk former and specifically within former build in this instance and what actually
happens is that with all the information and context that's provided in tools such as REVIT when it
comes to former build the GCs on site and the subcontractors are needed to input information on these
material deliveries and other workings on site and input information manually and so between
let's take the materials as the example between all the data that's input and all the issues
of risks that are being flagged not only is that reliant on manual efforts and trust within the
supply chain to input at the right time there is there is also no cross check that's happening
between these two systems at least not in a streamlined way as a this is exactly why we have these
data drop-offs between phases and why information is lost and we don't have the continuity that helps
prevent rework and ultimately support the overall delivery efficiency of delivery on site
where QFIRS system comes into this and how this how this manifests within the Autodesk ecosystem
is that QFIRS designs to be that sense check in between by helping teams to better and easily capture
data on all those materials without having the manual effort to input to different systems
and also to do their sense check on whether those original designs are actually aligned with
the deliveries that are arriving and the materials and the materials compliance around them
and so as a QFIRO can support this process in a much more easy fashion to take us from that
and Autodesk to a more digital one and better surface some of these insights
in terms of the data that's extracted from some of these documents and just describe a little
bit more around that the information is contained on the deliberated as themselves will give
basic information on the item that's arrived the description of the material the quantities
the subcontractors or suppliers that that comes from and maybe some addresses within there as well
what QFIRS aim to do in the first instance is to extract as much content and information from
those documents as we can without manual intervention and then automatically enrich that data
by understanding the relevant certificates that might be associated with those suppliers that
have been named to understand the carbon and calculate the carbon associated with those journeys
as well as the materials and also as you can see from the bottom of the screen here to make that
link make that connection between the material specification and this delivered item that has
now arrived on the job site and so all of this rich contextual information on the right hand side
is all extracted automatically simply from a photograph that's taken off the paper ticket or an
emailed copy in of the electronic ticket
that's the first layer the second layer then comes in to assess where those discrepancies exist
between that original material requirement or design requirement and of course what's actually
arrived so it's this intelligence layer that really supports teams and then being able to act to
try and prevent that mismatch material or that prohibited substance or supplier from being implemented
within or material from being installed within an again project
as though the benefits behind this and behind what a system like key flow in the AI that
underpins it can do is do automatically highlight these risks prevent those mistakes and also support
with that rich data the embodied carbon the transport carbon that exists within this material's
applied chain and by preventing rework prevent the unnecessary wastage that then happens on these jobs
as well so the integration with form and build and what that looks like is a relatively straightforward
connection between deploying Qflow initially on job sites and capturing the data on those materials
through these photographs through these emailed copies and then Qflow during the heavy lifting
of extracting that text keeping that data within the Qflow system and then taking out the highlighted
risks and issues that exist and sending those directly into form a build so that those issues can
then be can then trigger other actions within the form of ecosystem in terms of how those risks
can be managed and again better aligning the better aligning the design intent from systems that
just rev it with the reality of what's been installed and delivered within form a build.
Beyond this we're hoping to create an additional intelligence there where more information
insights that are surfaced from the data being collected within Qflow will manifest within
form a build and other areas within the Autodesk ecosystem as well as directly putting information
on design intent from different Autodesk systems such as rev it to then career at the
complete closed workflow between what we designed what's been installed and what's been delivered
the discrepancies and then learning from that moving forwards as well.
And so to paint this into context within the form a ecosystem this is specifically focused on
the build phase so by leveraging data from one system and incorporating that within the form a
ecosystem it means that other actions can then be triggered beyond the raw data itself so
being able to assign tasks to who's going to be addressing those risks and managing the material
supply chain supporting with learning this within Autodesk own AI from that same data and how can
we use that to again better reform new projects in terms of the potential risks and
and other learnings that can support program delivery in the longer term.
So all of this is very much around how can we again as Martin mentioned earlier as well
better connect data between these phases so we don't have the stop-offs and that we can actually
deliver the outcomes of these projects that we're intending to.
So I'll bring us back to this view on ideal state and where we're heading.
Currently things are disconnected and it results in things like rework and waste and general
project risk but we're developing solutions for that improved data continuity and addressing
interoperability between solutions and external ecosystems of data and services.
Qflow is a great example because with these types of integrations we can expand to other
sources of data sources of services and improve data connectivity and data traceability
that will ultimately help achieve more sustainable outcomes.
The Qflow solution really supports the Autodesk vision by enabling this continuity between people
processes and technology.
I think this ties on incredibly nicely when we think about all this data and the context of the
Gauntwred. Now of course for those of you who are familiar the Gauntwred is a specific requirement.
There's come out the Building Safety Act in the UK but ultimately the principles are
widely applicable to all kinds of different construction activities where we need to have this
consistent flow between between the original plans and specifications and any decisions that
are taken throughout the duration of a project that can help avoid in this case the risk
detents who are then utilising other buildings or residential spaces or otherwise.
But also in general beyond the health and safety implications of having
better data continuity to Martha's point around the other sustainability outcomes.
This isn't just around preventing the avoidable errors and the costs or liability associated
with that. It is also around preventing the unnecessary wasteage and costs associated with this as well.
At the moment we already know for example that 14% of materials arriving on different
job sites are going directly into waste and so there are direct margin implications on
live job sites to making sure that we have that continuity of data and the continuity of
information to help make the best decisions and to help make sure that we're delivering
an accordance with what we set out to.
And so time is back to the original example we heard at the beginning around the
simplistic problem of the wrong aggregate being installed and the problems that created
with systems that can operate together such as Qflow and Autodesk former.
What we see happening is an avoidance of these issues that are being raised and avoidance
of the risk of rework and delays to the program and other implications on the project
and ultimately avoiding the 1 million claim that can come about from again a very simple problem
that in this instance can actually be tackled with quite a simple solution involving the
connectivity between these two systems. So thank you very much for listening and we hope you
enjoy the rest of the sessions.
